sentences.text: OK, I just want to ask for a minute, can I?
Can I ask you?
And I'll tell you when.
OK.
Yeah.
Yeah-- should I mention before--
Yeah, before I left for Chkalov, I got malaria-- and it was a terrible sickness.
It was shaking before, and then you get terrible fever.
And then I started going out a lot, and I was almost like that.
But we had a doctor, a Polish guy.
His name was Prentki.
And he used to come.
As much money we had, we gave him, and then he used to come, and he gave me a lot of sulfur.
And I was cured, and then I went to Chkalov.
Yes.
I'm back in the market.
And I walk closer, and I want to see who is that brave guy that all these people run away that he was so brave to tell them to go.
So I'm looking, and that's the guy.
That's the guy that I met on the train.
So of course, it was a big happen as we saw each other.
So hand in hand--
He had come to look for you.
Yes.
He came to look-- he-- he remembered, I gave him the address and where we are and everything.
And he find me probably on the market, because all the people were on the market.
Maybe he asked people around what happened.
And we went to a park.
There was a nice park for a while ago from the market, and we sit there.
And we decided that we're getting married.
Whatever will happen, we'd be struggling together, living together, dying together, whatever is happen.
Then I was wondering, what's going to happen when I come and tell my sister [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that I want to get married and things like that?
And her husband came then from the-- from the war where they took him.
And when we came into the house, my husband and her husband from the same city, from [NON-ENGLISH].
And he told me right away from which family he comes and who he is.
And he's not married, and he's so and so, you know.
And then so we had already witness that I'm not marrying somebody who's got a wife, and then when I come back, we're going to find a wife with children or something like that.
That was always- A lot of people did it, and then they were sorry, because they had to separate later when they came back.
So we went into the [NON-ENGLISH] they call it.
It's like the city hall in here.
And for three ruble, we got married, and we still lived with-- with them, together.
But it was very difficult.
It was a small room, and we couldn't-- so I decide, and I said to my husband, look, let's go out.
And he start working in a place where they used to bring corn to make flour.
So they brought it from other provinces, and he went into work, [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it.
And that was in Samarkand.
Yes.
Yes-- yeah.
So we left them, and we went to where the train comes in, and I found one small room with a Uzbek woman, with a child, there.
And I had my little room in there.
And he used to work in the [NON-ENGLISH] for the-- there was a place that used to bring the--
Grain, and then it went I don't know where.
So a lot of people were working in that.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I got pregnant, you know, while we were living there, and I got a miscarriage.
So in the middle of the night, he had to rush me, and after that, I got a big fever.
Because the war was going on, the bomb was-- was bad.
And there was no light or anything.
And they didn't do a good job.
And I got very sick, and I was also with fever.
But I recovered, and I came back.
And he was still working.
And then I gave birth to my daughter, and that was already 1945.
And the war was finished in '46.
And I went to a hospital to have her.
In the meantime, I sent out letters all over-- if anybody's alive.
So when I gave birth to my daughter, my husband came in the morning, and he brought a letter with all the names of my family who is alive, all these sisters.
That was Chiel was Henyik, and Chavche was Hella, because they give Polish names.
Freidke was Fella, and Usher was killed.
Boyfriends grabbed him-- when-- that's going to be later the story there when I talk about them.
And I was very happy that my daughter brought me such happiness, because I received a letter that I have somebody alive.
And we were living Samarkand.
In the meantime, my husband got very sick, and he got asthma and something very terrible-- big spots on his whole body.
And they told me to take out the baby from there, the doctor, because she's going to get it-- it's typhus.
Like they said, it's typhus they thought.
So I just take a schmatta like that and made a bed outside of mine-- of my little room.
And the winds were terrible with the sand.
They went into your eyes and to all over.
And I tried to save him, because the Russian, what they did, if you had typhus, they took you to the hospital, and they give you cold beds, and you went.
Because they had too much people sick, they couldn't cure them.
And I found the Jewish doctor, and he came, and he find out that he ate something-- an egg or something, and it was stale.
And that did it to him.
And he gave him to-- you know.
There was no-- no drug store.
We had to go private in the pharmacy.
If you had somebody where they were making the drugs, and I had somebody.
My girlfriend's brother was there working.
So we saved him, and we were living there with another few families also from summer camp.
And then they start thinking of going back, that you want to go back.
And you had to have a lot of documentation.
They took everything away from me.
I had only one paper from grade seven that I graduate, mine diploma, and 1,000 times they wanted to take it away.
And I saved it, because if I didn't have that, I couldn't come back home, because they didn't believe.
They said, you are Russian.
You're not born in Poland.
What can you show us?
So whatever you had, they took it away.
Later, they claimed they want some more, because they didn't want us to leave.
They want us over there.
But we registered somehow.
It took us a long time, and it was very, very-- you know.
And God helped that one day, we were packed in the wagons, and we couldn't take anything with, whatever you had-- money or something.
Or this ring was a heavy ring, which we had got married, and my husband had one like that.
So when I came to Canada, I made up this ring, a signet, you call it, something like that.
And my daughter have it.
She wants to have it for memory.
My husband, his, also-- so we made-- bought other rings, and we made it like that.
So my other daughter keeps it.
She wants to have a souvenir from that.
And they put us in the trains, and that we're going back.
And it was-- still the war was going on, but they were pushing back.
That was in 1946, starting with.
And we came under-- they put us in the trains with food-- with no food, and we were starting to come closer to Bratslav, where we were going.
There was [NON-ENGLISH], they called-- these people from the partisan but Goyim-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And they came from all the sides from the bushes, and they took the driver.
They told the driver to stop the train, and we had a lot of Lubavitch people.
They were coming with us, too.
They also wanted to leave.
And they went from train to train.
They started from the back, and whatever you have, and then they start killing the people.
So whatever you had, and they had-- give it to them.
And so my husband and another few guys, whoever was-- they organized right away.
The wives and the kids off the train, off the train.
And in the bushes, we went in the corn.
It was at night, in the middle of the night, whoever could.
Because the back one, they robbed.
They killed.
They did everything.
But we went, we hide.
And then this guy-- these guys, my husband and all this, they took sticks from the bushes.
And they went to the first wagon, and they told to the guy, the conductor, if you're not going to go now, right now, we're going to kill you here.
And he got scared.
In the meantime, a few were left there to hold him, that he couldn't go.
This guy is robbed, and they went in the bushes, and the other guys came to pick us up.
It went fast in the train, and whoever-- oh, and when they killed, they took off the-- the wagons with the-- they took him off.
They disconnected those cars.
They disconnected them, so what was left, maybe a half a train.
And we went in, and they started-- the guy, the conductor, you go.
Otherwise, you'll be killed.
And he went, and he went so fast, and we were, I don't know, a few days, and we came to Bratslav.
We came to Bratslav, and there, we came into a big house, where all the people were coming from all over, and everybody would share everything.
And in Bratslav, I found my family.
So it's the first time I found my brother and all of them.
And they were living in a-- the German disappeared.
And they were living in a big German big house like an apartment.
An apartment.
And I went to live with them.
Then I found my sister after she was married, and she had a little boy, what he is in Israel, Josi.
And my cousin, Chanale-- she was going for a doctor.
She had a guy who was in love with her, he want to take her.
But he went before, she still left.
And Freidke was there too, Fella.
And they dressed us, they helped us, because they were already before a little bit.
They came before to [NON-ENGLISH].
And then we decided we don't want to be there, and we want to go to Israel.
Well, this when I came-- when I came to Israel for the first time, 1967, my brother and my family, they told me everything what happened there with them.
So we decided that we want to go to Israel.
And from there, we came to Austria.

We also, at night, we smuggled the Czechoslovakia border and everything, and we came to-- we came to-- from Czechoslovakia to-- it was a terrible journey at nights with the-- with the-- with the small kids.
We slept in the bushes, and we were just going, walking through the-- through the things.
The Bricha came along, and they told us, you say to everyone that you Greeks.
Because they don't want the Russians to know that we-- and I had my passport.
That's why I don't get anything from the Russians.
Because I had my passport.
They brought it here.
The other one, they threw everything away.
And they didn't say-- if they ask, who are you?
What are you?
We said, we don't know.
We're Greeks.
The Bricha told us this, to do that.
And they took us, the United Nations-- UNRRA, we call it.
They brought food already.
We went from place to place, and they kept us going.
In Austria, there was the concentration camps, where they kept Jewish people from before.
So when we came there, we saw it still-- [INAUDIBLE].
On the way before we came, we stopped the train.
[SOBBING] We all went and picked up hair, and what do you call this?
Braids, hands, clothes, where the German couldn't-- they just-- and they were all open, and we had the Lubavitch.
And we digged a big grave, and we covered them all with our hands, with things, and we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prayed [SOBBING] and said Kaddish and kept going.
It was terrible experience.
Then when we came to Austria, and the Bricha put us in this places where the Germans kept all the Jewish people from all over the world.
And we slept in that barracks-- 10 families, five families.
We're just a little over in the middle.
And we used to go every morning for food.
We couldn't cook.
We didn't do anything.
My daughter was small.
She couldn't eat.
She was like a finger.
She was already almost a year, a year, maybe old.
And I used to go to-- in Vienna-- in Austria, sell what the Americans used to give us-- a bit of chocolate and things like that to sell it in case to buy for her an egg or food.
And every morning, we would get up, and they'd give us milk, you know, and-- and my husband right away went to the-- like, they had a committee.
There is one woman.
She's here.
Her husband was the lagerfeuhrer, they called him, [NON-ENGLISH].
Start coming clothes and things from America, and they start giving us clothes, and dressing us up and things like that.
But it was very tragic in this concentration where we lived, because you find all kinds-- it was very scared.
We find spoons, we find shoes. we found from all these people when they were in there, where they were tortured, where they were taken from there to the oven someplace.
You know?
So it was very scary and terrible to live, and they organized.
Then the Bricha came, and they organized that they can take us to Italy, but we have to walk.
So in this concentration camp was kitchens, so the very religious people came there, and they took over all the kitchens.
And they wouldn't let us in, because we're not religious.
So we had to make fire outside and cook for the kids anything.
They ask us-- so when we were going to climb on the-- on the-- on the heights, on the-- Mountains?
--on the mountains on the-- Italy, and my husband was one of them.
That- he helped to blankets.
They came from-- they gave us blankets for everybody who came.
And every night, the people were taking through the-- like from the camps to Italy, Milano.
And from there, somehow to Israel, whatever it was.
And we were still sitting there, and we were ready already also to go, and my husband had a sister in here.
And good enough, we wrote there.
She received somehow the letters, and they made us papers to come.
But in the meantime, we wouldn't need it, because the Jewish Congress brought from the people whoever is a dressmaker, whoever is-- and they came the register, and they went out.
They all went, and we were still there.
But we were sitting in Austria, and Veksheit was our big place.
Then because my husband helped so much, we got rooms in Bindermichel.
It was a really nice apartment, also from the German, a little bit nicer, and we used to have already enjoyment, something like that.
And from there, we came.
My husband was very ashamed that we should have gone to Israel, because he was the leader.
He wouldn't want to think about something else, and now is the time.
Oh, and while we were in Veksheit-- I have a picture-- that all the countries agreed that we should have Israel, and we almost got crazy from being happy that, you know, that everybody vote for them.
But we went out, and we came March the 20, 1949.
We came to Canada.
Now where else should I go?
I have to go back.
We have to go back about your family and what they went through.
I came to Canada.
My sister-in-law [INAUDIBLE] was here.
They treat us very nice, and my husband went to work in a factory, and he was a very fast worker, and life was good.
And I rent two rooms.
I lived with an old lady.
I was like her daughter.
She was sick, and I helped her.
It was Bellevue, 36 Bellevue here in Toronto.
And little by little, we saved up money, and I bought my first house on Markham Street.
And then we went to Indian Road.
We had a little grocery, and the kids were growing.
But I didn't want to be on Indian Road, because my daughters were going to a non- a Gentile school.
And I was afraid she was going to fall in love.
She was very-- played and singed, and I was afraid she'd fall in love with a shegetz.
So I figure out, and we bought on Badgers a building, and we opened up our own business.
It wasn't too good.
It was very hard and very terrible, but we were happy that we're in Canada in a new country, and we have our own country, Israel, and we have something to live for.
[SOBBING]
And I appreciate every minute that I came to such a beautiful country, and everybody gave us a hand.
And I never suffered.
I never had no food, and I appreciate everything-- my house, my life, my husband, my kids.
I could give them education.
I could live with Jewish people.
I went to shuls.
I could join a lot-- choirs.
I was doing social work for all kind of clubs, and in 1967, I decided I wanted to go meet my family after being so long.
So I went to Israel for the first time, and the war was going on there on the border, and I was afraid to go.
And my husband says, what are your life better than theirs?
You shouldn't be afraid.
You go take a gun and shoot just the way they are.
I said, I'm going to leave you here with the kids.
He said, if you don't want to go, I go.
You go, because we couldn't leave the kids with them, and then the second time, he went.
And I came to Israel, and then I can't tell you the reunion.
The first thing, when I came to Israel, and I went out from the airplane, my feet were numb.
I couldn't walk, because in Europe, every cheder, when we were sitting, my father with tears used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
When is going to come the day that we live to see Jerusalem?
And I said, I wasn't-- I was a kid still.
I wasn't religious yet.
I didn't understand what Jerusalem means.
And he was praying with so much heart that he wanted to see Jerusalem.
So God, kill them all.
Let me live.
Save me from so many times that I could have been killed and dead, and I was so young, and I didn't understand.
I didn't I didn't shame myself.
I didn't go to live with any guys.
I had always my head on the right side.
I knew what I can do, what can I not do.
I was talking to myself.
I was preaching to myself.
So I am the holy one that I have the right to step in that holy place.
I took off my shoes, and I kissed the earth.
[SOBBING] I said, I am blessed.
Am I a holy person or something?
To come to Israel and to be the first one there, and I can tell you when I came and I saw my family, and we-- the first time that we met, and-- and I got so rich that I have a family.
And they all got married, and they have kids and everything.
And I stayed with them.
Since then, we went about nine times, and I went with my husband, and 80- '89 was the last trip when my husband took sick.
He started getting sick.
We couldn't go, but being there, I find out what they went through, and this is the last chapter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first thing, my brother took me to the synagogue.
He was a cantor in the Ramat Gan in the new, big synagogue.
In Israel, a cantor is not paid, it's just honorable.
That he was davening, and he had a beautiful voice.
So he took me to the synagogue, where he was davening.
And I sit on-- with the religious one, I sit on the top with the women.
So he went up to to daven-- he davened special with all these melodies when we were davening in Europe and our shtiebel when we had.
And we helped and everything.
And I cried.
I never in my life for happiness, and the way he was davning, you know?
And then we took ourselves with the hands, and we went and told stories.
And I said, I want to know-- I want to know, how did you survive?
But a little bit, I find out in Bratslav, when I came to Bratslav, and I was with them awhile, and they told me how they survived.
When the war started, my father wasn't there, but my mother was there and all the kids.
They made under a chicken little house, what do you call it?
A bunker.
They were sitting there day and night, but when it came Yom Kippur, after Yom Kippur, they were sitting, and they had nothing to eat, and the house was just a few shacks away.
And there was my auntie with her kids too.
Chanale is the doctor's mother there.
And some other people that I don't know-- neighbors.
Oh, and between that, when the war started, and they were with Hitler, my sister, Chavcha, she had a girlfriend, and her name was Salka in Polish.
She took my whole family-- about 60 people.
She had them for six weeks in-- hidden by her in another-- in a little-- in a farm.
I don't remember the place where that was.
And later, it got bad, and they had to go.
Just a minute.
That was-- that was with them when they were hiding.
When they were hiding, it was after Yom Kippur-- Yom Kippur.
After Yom Kippur night, my mother says she's going to go out.
Maybe something's left in the house.
She's going to bring anything, some water just to keep them going.
She went out, and Usher went with her, my brother went with her.
And as she won out, the Polack, somebody saw her, reported right away to the German.
And they didn't take 10 minutes.
And they knew that already.
They saw that.
So my mother went hiding under-- under the house.
It was actually not a house.
It was also something for chickens.
I don't know.
And my brother climbed up on the wall, on the roof.
On the roof.
And he flat lay.
And they pulled my mother out.
They couldn't find him, and they shot her right on the spot.
And she had a lot of jewelry, whatever we had from-- the golden watch, whatever we had from the house.
Everything she had hidden on her back in there.
And there's everything they didn't.
But when they saw the kids later, they got very scared.
And they came out of this bunker, and then all of them, everybody around to a different place.
And my auntie went also out, and they caught her with her little son.
And they shot them, too, but one of the daughters, Rifka, she went with mine family.
Rifka and Chanale, they were hiding in them, and they pulled them in.
And when they came to-- they went to hide someplace.
So they found a place by a Gentile guy.
He was the worst Jewish enemy, and I remember his name was Szczur.
Szczur is something with a mouse in Polish, but the bigger one, the rat.
A rat.
His name was Szczur.
He was a terrible anti-Semite.
But they dug out there, and they were sitting there in the winter in the cold.
They used to go out, and my brother, Usher, was-- he-- all the friends were there.
My neighbors in Poland, my father used to take Friday and bring them challahs and fish.
And this guy was the one, he took care on that district.
And we were brought up together with the kids.
And when my brother went out, he went from thar-- under earth-- to find a piece of bread or something.
That Szczur spotted him.
He was going to whatever he can find-- potatoes, raw potatoes in there.
And he ran after him, and he threw his jacket behind his feet.
And he couldn't run so fast.
And he grabbed him.
He grabbed him.
He brought him to the German.
That was exactly about maybe a few weeks before they were finished with the war.
And so he give away to the German, and he got for that a kilo of sugar and some butter from the German.
And the German killed him on the spot.
My other brother was living in Siedlice.
He was married, and he had two or three kids, beautiful kids.
When it was bad in the big cities, he came also to them to live.
But he hide-- he hide all over.

And the last time he was hiding if not in our place-- we had cows and things like that too-- he came into that stable there, and there was to go up on the ladder, because we kept the food and straw and everything on the top for the cows.
They went up over there, all of them, and they laid there, and they were quiet.
Just at night, somebody could go up.
If they throw something for the cows or something, they went down, and they eat it.
And somebody noticed they are there, or the owner for the-- I don't know what it was, because the Polacks took over everything our house.
They took everything over.
We were very rich people.
We had land, and we had orchards, and we did all kinds of business.
And they took everything over.
Like we had a Sefer Torah in there, and candelabras.
And the candelabras are in Israel.
My sisters got it.
She wanted to give it to me, my brother, actually.
They're huge-- I should bring 'em here and put it in the museum.
And I said that Israel is the proper place, because Israel will never go under, [SOBBING] I said.
And this is the place to keep all these things that are ours.
They saved some-- all kinds of holy books and things like that they brought with them.
And I said, I can't take it back to the goyshka country.
I said, OK, if I would have it here, I'll give it here.
I'll give it [NON-ENGLISH] someplace in there.
But I didn't let him.
And he established in Israel a place where-- where-- for the [NON-ENGLISH], from our city.
And I put my names of my parents on there, and-- you have to stop?
We have to stop for a short break.
OK.
It's come to end already.
